title: Macroprudential Policy and the Revolving Door of Risk: Lessons from Leveraged Lending Guidance

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This May 2017 Federal Reserve Bank of New York staff report examines the U.S. interagency guidance on leveraged lending and finds that while the guidance reduced banks' leveraged lending activity, it primarily affected large, closely supervised banks and triggered a migration of leveraged lending to nonbank entities. The research highlights the 'revolving door of risk' phenomenon and questions whether the guidance accomplished its broader goal of reducing systemic financial risk.
key_points: Interagency leveraged lending guidance primarily impacted large, closely supervised banks, but only after supervisors issued important clarifications
The guidance triggered a migration of leveraged lending from banks to nonbanks
Nonbanks did not exhibit more lax lending policies than banks, but did increase their bank borrowing following guidance issuance
The guidance was effective at reducing banks' leveraged lending activity
It is unclear whether the guidance accomplished its broader goal of reducing systemic risk to financial stability
Authors: Sooji Kim, Matthew C. Plosser, and João A.C. Santos
why_it_matters: This research documents how macroprudential regulation can produce regulatory arbitrage as lending activity migrates from regulated banks to less-regulated nonbank entities. For market structure analysis, it demonstrates that supervisory monitoring is critical for policy effectiveness and that the 'revolving door of risk' poses a fundamental challenge to macroprudential regulation. The finding that nonbank lending growth was financed by increased bank borrowing suggests interconnections that may undermine the risk-reduction intent of such guidance.
macro_context: This research contributes to understanding how macroprudential policy tools interact with market structure. It documents unintended consequences of regulatory guidance on the allocation of credit across bank and nonbank sectors.
market_structure_context: Core finding: leveraged lending guidance caused migration from supervised banks to less-regulated nonbanks, but nonbank growth was partially financed by increased bank borrowing. This illustrates how regulatory boundaries affect the flow of credit and risk across the financial system.

human_review.notes: Historical research paper from 2017 that provides academic context for understanding macroprudential policy effectiveness and regulatory arbitrage in leveraged lending markets. While not a current market-moving document, it is relevant background for market structure analysis regarding bank vs. nonbank credit allocation.

notes: Source is a staff research report (Staff Report No. 815) from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York. Documented as 'available only in PDF' with 40 pages. Published May 2017 - classified as historical record. The research examines the interagency leveraged lending guidance as a case study in macroprudential policy effectiveness. Key finding on 'revolving door of risk' describes how risk migrates across regulatory boundaries. Original crawling metadata indicates high domain trust tier.
